name: Supersymmetry (Wess-Zumino algebra)
summary: The foundational principle that the Poincare algebra is extended by anticommuting (fermionic) generators Q_alpha so that bosons and fermions are unified into common representations. Cancels quadratic divergences in scalar masses (the principal motivation), produces a graviton's superpartner (the gravitino), and constrains low-energy phenomenology severely. Distinct from MSSM in being the underlying symmetry rather than a specific implementation; all SUSY mechanisms in the catalog (MSSM, AMSB, GMSB, RPV, super-WIMP) build on this foundation.

addresses_puzzles.note: Boson-fermion mass cancellation removes the quadratic divergence to scalar masses. Whether SUSY is realised at low scales (and thus addresses the EW hierarchy at observable energies) is a phenomenological question — LHC nulls have constrained low-scale realisations.
SUSY at sub-GUT scales protects the Higgs quartic from running negative; in supersymmetric theories the Higgs lambda is bounded from below by gauge couplings, eliminating EW vacuum metastability.

touches_benchmarks.note: Generic SUSY phases produce 1-loop EDMs at observable levels; the SUSY CP problem is the requirement that these phases be small.

introduces.note: SUSY as a symmetry is parameter-free; specific implementations (MSSM, NMSSM, etc.) introduce the parameters.

history: Wess and Zumino (1974), 'Supergauge transformations in four dimensions', Nucl. Phys. B 70, 39 — established the foundational supersymmetry algebra in 4D. Earlier work by Golfand-Likhtman (1971) and Volkov-Akulov (1972) preceded the WZ-algebra formulation. Foundational for ~50 years of BSM theory; Coleman-Mandula and Haag-Lopuszanski-Sohnius theorems show SUSY is the unique extension of Poincare allowed by S-matrix consistency.

references.citation: Wess and Zumino, 'Supergauge transformations in four dimensions', Nucl. Phys. B 70, 39 (1974)
